title: Brightness Constancy Assumption
concept: The brightness constancy assumption states that a pixel's color/intensity remains constant as it moves between frames.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What do the variables 'u' and 'v' represent in optical flow?

options.text: Horizontal and vertical pixel velocity vectors
Height and width of the input camera

exercises.explain: Yes! u and v are the horizontal and vertical velocity components of a pixel.

cards.title: The chameleon problem
cards.body: How does a computer know that a pixel in Frame 1 is the exact same pixel in Frame 2? If the object changed from bright red to dark blue instantly, tracking it would be impossible. To solve this, computer vision makes a fundamental assumption.

cards.title: Brightness Constancy
cards.body:
The brightness constancy assumption states that the intensity (brightness) of a pixel does not change as it moves from one frame to the next:
Intensity(x, y, t) = Intensity(x + u, y + v, t + 1)
This gives us a starting point to track pixels.

cards.title: A rolling colored ball
cards.body: If you roll a yellow tennis ball across a floor, the ball stays yellow. Individual patches of the ball don't change color as they roll. Because the yellow color remains constant, you can track the ball's movement across your room.

cards.title: When the assumption fails
cards.body: Imagine filming a mirror reflecting a candle. As the mirror tilts, the bright reflection slides across the glass. The physical glass didn't move, but the changing light makes the algorithm think the mirror itself is sliding.

cards.title: Not a perfect physical rule
cards.body: In the real world, light changes! Clouds block the sun, shadows cast, and highlights flicker. While brightness constancy is rarely 100% true, it is a necessary approximation that allows classical optical flow equations to be solved.

cards.title: Ideal vs. Real-World Light
cards.body: Comparing ideal brightness constancy with real-world scenarios:

left.text:
Moving pixel color stays constant
Easy mathematical tracking
No sudden lighting changes

right.text:
Shadows, reflections, and sun flares
Pixels change intensity as they move
Can confuse simple tracking equations

exercises.prompt: What is the core idea of the Brightness Constancy Assumption?

options.text: A moving pixel keeps the same brightness/intensity from frame to frame
A video camera must always record in bright daylight
All pixels in a frame must have identical color values
The camera's flashlight must stay turned on

exercises.explain: Correct! It assumes that when a pixel moves, its intensity value remains constant.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Brightness constancy is always 100% true in real-world videos.
exercises.explain: False. Real-world changes like shadows, highlights, and flickering lights frequently violate this assumption.

exercises.sentence: Sudden lighting changes can violate the brightness constancy assumption and cause ___ tracking errors.
exercises.explain: Changes in lighting can be misinterpreted as physical movement, leading to optical flow errors.

exercises.prompt: Which of these events would violate the brightness constancy assumption?

options.text: A cloud passing in front of the sun, dimming the entire scene
A car driving forward at a constant speed in a well-lit studio
A static book resting on a table

exercises.explain: A passing cloud changes the illumination level of the entire scene, violating the brightness constancy assumption.

exercises.prompt: A model is tracking a white car driving from a bright sunny street into a dark tunnel. What challenge does the optical flow algorithm face?

options.text: The pixels representing the car become darker, violating brightness constancy and potentially breaking the tracker.
The car will automatically speed up inside the model's memory.
The camera resolution will instantly drop to zero.
The car will turn into a grayscale bicycle.

exercises.explain: Exactly! As the car enters the tunnel, the light level drops. The pixels change intensity, which violates the constancy assumption and can cause the math to lose track of the car. Next: how do we solve the optical flow equations in practice?
